preamble: The shift from the voiceless postalveolar affricate [tʃ] to the high-front vowel [i] is a form of vocalization. This typically occurs in unstressed positions or suffixes, where the consonant loses its stop and fricative components, leaving only its palatal vowel quality.

examples.note: The final [tʃ] was elided; the remaining vowel [i] lengthened and underwent the Great Vowel Shift. The [i] in modern 'I' comes from the original vowel, not from vocalization of [tʃ].
The final affricate was lost (elision); the preceding vowel [i] survived. Strictly this is ch→∅ with vowel retention, rather than ch→i vocalization.
Reported in certain dialectal developments where palatal affricates reduced to glides or vowels; specific dialect not verified.

sources: Baugh, A. C. & Cable, T. (2013). A History of the English Language (6th ed.). Routledge.
Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction. MIT Press.
